第一次抵达曼彻斯特，却没有陌生感。
很多城市是先抵达，再认识。曼彻斯特似乎相反。还没来之前，早已在转播画面里、在英剧里、在电影里反复见过它。足球，音乐，工业。这座城市的标签太多，多到真正走在街上时，反而有一种奇妙的错位——老旧的路面，红砖墙，偶尔有些脏乱的街角，便利店门口定期刷新的流浪汉。好像和之前认识的那个曼彻斯特，不太一样。
曼彻斯特有两支世界级的球队。所以关于这座城市的第一印象，似乎永远绕不开那个问题：这里的天空，到底是什么颜色？
我和曼城的缘分，说起来并不浪漫。
最早只是在 FIFA游戏 里发现这支球队的转会预算异于常人，顺手选了它。后来一用，就是很多年。也许那时候还有一点年轻人的倔强——当所有人都在谈论红魔曼联时，偏偏想看看这座城市另一边的蓝色。没想到，一个游戏里的随手选择，最后会变成十多年后真实抵达 Etihad 的理由。
这支球队在过去常常被叫作暴发户、土豪球队。
直到那个男人。
几个月前申请英签时，我在 cover letter 里写：作为十多年的曼城球迷，希望来 Etihad 看一场比赛，也许还能赶上瓜帅执教的最后一个主场。那时候，这句话多少只是一个理由。起初订机票和球票时，想的甚至还是"万一夺冠了呢"。但赛季最后几轮的形势急转直下，争冠的希望一点一点消失，关于瓜帅离队的消息越来越密集。于是这趟旅程的主题，也跟着变了。
不是来看一次冠军加冕。
是来送别一个时代。
国家足球博物馆 博物馆旁边就是曼彻斯特大教堂。教堂后面，有一座纪念 2017 年 Manchester Arena 恐袭遇难者的 Glade of Light。
恰巧是 5 月 22 日，周年纪念日。市政人员前来献花，悼念。那一刻很难不意识到：足球、音乐、城市创伤和公共记忆，在曼彻斯特，并不是分开的东西。
所以几个小时之后，当看到 Pep 在告别词里提起 Manchester Arena attack 时，对我而言，那并不只是官方演讲里的煽情段落。那是这座城市背后的东西。
“Remember, the Manchester Arena attack, when this city showed the world what strength actually looks like? Not anger. Not fear. Just love. Community. Togetherness. A city united.”
博物馆，上午十点开门。十点不到，门口已经站着几个来自世界各地的球迷。
不像英国大部分免费的博物馆，这座是要买票的。
一楼更像传统意义上的博物馆，越往上走，则越像互动体验、亲子娱乐和大众文化空间。1863 年最早的足球规则，1966 年世界杯的藏品，1872 年英格兰对苏格兰——世界第一场正式国际比赛——的球衣。
但这个博物馆真正有意思的地方，不在于它只收藏伟大球星或伟大比赛。
它也收藏球迷文化。工人阶级文化。丝带徽章，帽子，围巾，比赛日的小物件。那些不被正史留意的东西，安静地躺在玻璃柜里，像在说：足球从来不只是足球。它是身份认同，是社区，是人与人的共同纽带。
Stadium Tour 去 Etihad 的路并不复杂。
曼彻斯特不像伦敦那样依赖庞大的地铁系统。市区更多是地面上的 tram（这一词源于最早在17世纪英国的矿车轨道）。
开放式站台没有闸机，tap in、tap out 全靠自觉，也很容易忘记。
Tour 开始前，屏幕上又放了一遍瓜帅的告别视频。明明已经看过，但在球场里再看，还是很难不被击中。
同行的，大多是家长带着孩子。像一种传承。
解说员先带大家在南看台坐下，讲球场和球队的历史。然后是 Tunnel Club、球员入场处、更衣室、采访区、球员通道、教练席。9320 那一场的联赛冠军奖杯和比赛用球，欧洲超级杯和世俱杯奖杯，还有刚刚被命名为 Pep Guardiola Stand 的北看台。
球场外的雕像，尤其像时间分层。
西边 Colin Bell Stand 附近，是 Colin Bell、Francis Lee、Mike Summerbee 三人的铜像。他们代表曼城更早的冠军时代——现代资本和全球化叙事之前的旧曼城。东边，则是 Vincent Kompany、David Silva、Sergio Agüero。他们代表的是新曼城，是我这一代海外球迷真正熟悉的曼城。
也许再过不久，Pep 的雕像也会在这里。
Match Day 比赛日那天，市中心明显变蓝了。
平时就总能看到穿曼城球衣的人，到了 matchday 更是如此。开赛前三四个小时，St Peter’s Square 附近的 pub 门口早已坐满了球迷。大家喝着酒、唱着歌，为下午的比赛预热。对本地人来说，这也许只是又一个周末。对我这样的海外球迷来说，却像是朝圣。
普通席位很难买，关键场次还需要会员积分和抢票。海外球迷往往只能选二手票，或者 hospitality。于是我买了 Joe’s Bar 的款待票。入场时领到一本赛刊和一张饮品券，第一反应是换杯 Asahi——毕竟是赞助商。
等真正坐到位置上，我才意识到不妙。
下午场，东看台。太阳直射眼睛。再加上英国罕见破纪录的五月高温，观赛体验几乎从一开始就带着一点狼狈。我甚至因为坐在 TIFO 正下方，没能亲眼看到纪念 Pep 和几位功勋球员的巨型 TIFO。
曼城上了不少替补。像一套"下赛季不在了，就好好感受一次主场氛围"的阵容。上半场有机会，也进了球；下半场被维拉反击打得狼狈，丢球之后更显得疲惫。更像是这两个赛季的缩影。
可真正重要的，本来也不是比赛。
纪念仪式才是这一天的核心。老队长来了，埃德森来了，许多功勋退役球员也送来视频祝福。兔兔和蒋磊替补下场时，两队球员列队送别。那一刻很难不动容。足球世界当然残酷——球员来来去去，教练也终究会离开。
总是说夏天是告别的季节。何尝不是呢。
这当然是一家被资本重塑的现代俱乐部，也背负着许多争议。但在这些仪式里，你又能看到一种很具体的人情味。它没有把十年简单包装成奖杯数字，而是把一个个名字、一个个瞬间、一个个离开的背影，一个一个地，摆在你面前。
十年时间，说长也长，说短也短。
Pep 留下了二十座奖杯，也留下了一种关于"曼城应该如何踢球"的想象。也许会有新的球星、新的冠军，但这个时代结束时，还是会让人突然意识到一件事——
原来我们喜欢一支球队，喜欢的并不只是赢球。
散场后，大家往 tram 站走。似乎本地人更喜欢多走几公里路，而不是挤 tram。车厢里全是天蓝色球衣，大家都挺安静的。
也许是因为，一个时代真的结束了。
冠军巡游 Matchday 的第二天，仍然没有从昨日的告别里完全醒来。
巡游的起点在 Newton Street。严格来说，这是一次冠军巡游。但站在人群里，我总觉得它并不只是为了庆祝这个赛季的几座奖杯。昨天在 Etihad，是球场内部的告别——有掌声、视频、发言和眼泪。今天到了城市街头，告别被换成了另一种形式。
蓝色冷烟。气球。旗帜。歌声。
也许这就是足球城市最擅长的事。它不会让一个时代只在球场里结束，而是要把它搬到街上，让整座城市再送一次。
我特意站在巡游出发点附近，甚至挑了一个和官方摄像差不多的机位。想尽可能把这一天留下来。摁快门的手，几乎舍不得松开。
街边已经是一片天蓝色的海。几个 teenager 爬上红绿灯，只为了在人群上方看得更清楚——也让大家看得更清楚他们。
我在原地站了快一个小时，也和周围的人一起，把那些熟悉的歌，唱了一遍。
这些歌，平时隔着屏幕听，总觉得只是比赛背景音。真正站在人群里，才意识到它们更像是一种共同语言。你不需要认识身边的人，也未必说着同一种母语。只要旋律响起，大家就自然地进入同一个节奏。
昨天的告别更像仪式。今天的巡游更像狂欢。
只是狂欢，并不意味着没有伤感。它只是把伤感藏在了歌声和蓝烟里。
当巴士开动时，人群开始涌动。那一刻很难说，大家到底是在庆祝冠军，还是在感谢一个时代。
也许，两者本来就分不开。
一个时代结束了。
昨天我们在球场里说再见，今天曼彻斯特在街头又说了一次。
这一次，可能没有眼泪。
附：一些给后来者的 Tips 关于购票 普通场次可以注册 Matchday Membership 后从官方购票，但对于热门场次，往往需要会员积分，对于海外球迷并非友好，只能选择官方 hospitality、二级市场平台，或者旅行社/票务套餐
曼城官方指定二级市场Viagogo，实测靠谱
理论上票是实名制的，但没有人核查？
关于入场 只接受不大于 A4 的小型手提包，否则需要寄存
门票电子化，需要下载到apple wallet或者google wallet上，刷NFC入场
Hospitality 有着装要求，不能穿客队颜色服装，不能穿运动服和帽子（Joe’s Bar 除外）
关于 Hospitality 大致可分成几类，具体可见官网
Premium seats，93:20 Lounge；轻量 VIP / 入门 VIP，有专属 lounge/bar、padded seat，无专属通道，赛前 1.5 小时进入 lounge；Blocks 209–210 / 221–222；
Sports Bar 型，比如 Joe’s、KITS、Commonwealth Bar；有 padded seat、酒吧空间，部分包含 welcome drink、赛刊、 或中场饮品，但食物和酒水很多仍然需要另买；可提前2hr进场
Restaurant / Lounge 型，比如 The Mancunian、Citizens、Legends、Manager’s Corner。更强调餐食、桌位、现场娱乐
高端包厢 / Fine dining / Tunnel Club，如Asahi Super Dry Tunnel Club，迎接一线队、玻璃球员通道、5 道式季节菜单、香槟/鸡尾酒/烈酒/啤酒/葡萄酒全包、场边看热身、替补席后加热座位
超高端 / 独家体验，如Captain’s Centre Circle，开球前走上中圈与双方队长合影；包含 Tunnel Club Premier 权益
赛前或赛后 hospitality，如Heart of the City、Campus Post-Match，主打赛前赛后餐饮
关于 Stadium Tour 可在官网购票预约
建议把 Stadium Tour 安排在非比赛日，可以提前熟悉球场
球场外的雕像也不要错过
凭Stadium Tour凭证，可以在礼品商店享受9折优惠（当然大部分都是made in China）
新闻发布会有机会和瓜帅或者哈兰德的虚拟形象合影（以后就不知道了）
关于冠军巡游 前提是夺得冠军（不限于联赛冠军和男队）
一般将时间安排在赛季最后一场比赛后的bank holiday
具体行进路线关注官方网站和社交媒体（X）获取

title: 穿过看台与蓝色冷烟——英伦游记·足球篇：送别瓜迪奥拉

Prologue: From an Old Question to a New One I remember when I first started out in this industry, I followed a question on Zhihu:
“The internet has been fought to a bloody red ocean, yet smart manufacturing remains a wasteland — why won’t BAT (Baidu, Alibaba, Tencent) step into it?”
Over five years later, this question still has no definitive answer — but the times have changed.
Back then, the subtext was: technology is scarce, industries are waiting to be transformed, and as long as the top-tier players are willing to enter the fray, things will happen. Reality has since falsified that assumption in nearly every way imaginable. And now, LLMs have pushed the “technology is scarce” premise even further. But ask anyone pushing AI adoption in a traditional industry, and odds are they’ll tell you: it’s not that simple.
And so this old question takes on a new form in the AI era:
“AI is already this powerful — why can’t these industries get moving?”
Recently, the term FDE (Forward Deployed Engineer) has suddenly caught fire among major AI companies — and at its core, it’s a response to this very question: if the models are already powerful enough, then the “last mile” bottleneck must lie in the customer site, in the data, in the process, in the organization.
This article is my attempt to answer this old question, drawing on years of observation and hands-on practice.
Let me put the conclusion up front:
What most industries are missing right now isn’t AI — it’s X-Ops.
In other words, the question has never been “should we adopt AI?” — it’s whether this industry, this company, this production line, this workflow has been transformed into a system that AI can plug into, understand, participate in, and feed back into.
Why Has the Software Industry Been Hit Hardest by AI? It’s not AI. It’s DevOps.
If you look at the fields where AI has truly “broken in” over the past two years, the software industry itself appears to have taken the biggest hit. The reason the software industry has been hit hardest by AI isn’t that its practitioners are the smartest, nor that models happen to favor code — it’s that over the past two decades, it inadvertently transformed itself into the industry that’s easiest for AI to plug into.
AI-Friendliness Wasn’t Born This Way If I had to list the traits that put AI in its element within the software industry, here’s what I’d pick:
Open corpus, easy to access: GitHub
Short feedback cycles: Write code, compile, run tests, see results — feedback arrives in seconds or minutes
AI-friendly context: Code has comments, modules have interfaces, changes have diffs — nearly everything is textual and structured
Verifiable correctness: Unit tests, integration tests, CI pipelines provide an automatic grading mechanism for whatever the AI writes
These four pieces together set the iteration flywheel in motion: generate → verify → get feedback → fix → regenerate.
But go back to around the year 2000, and the software industry actually looked a lot more like today’s traditional industries. Release cycles were measured in years. A single version would be polished for months and shipped on a CD. What truly changed everything was the transformation of the delivery model brought by the internet. Software no longer went through “manufacture → copy → ship → install” — instead it became “develop → release → get feedback → update → re-release.” Once this closed loop took shape, the entire way the industry organized itself was forced to restructure:
Because you needed to release frequently, you needed version control (Git)
Because you needed to integrate frequently, you needed CI/CD
Because you needed to collaborate across teams, you needed code review, issue tracking, API documentation
Because things could go wrong in production at any moment, you needed logging, monitoring, alerting, observability
Because releasing carried high risk, you needed gradual rollout, rollback, A/B testing
Because collaboration kept scaling up, you needed Agile, DevOps, SRE (Site Reliability Engineering)
This entire apparatus is what we today collectively call DevOps. But the essence of DevOps isn’t a particular combination of tools — it’s an engineering culture that makes the entire process of “how software is produced, delivered, operated, and improved” explicit, versioned, automated, and observable.
The “Byproduct” DevOps Left Behind Became AI’s Food Here’s the most interesting part: DevOps was never designed for AI. It solved problems of human-to-human collaboration, the balance between quality and speed, the engineering challenge of delivering at scale.
But its byproducts almost perfectly hit every piece of infrastructure the AI era demands:
What DevOps left behind What it means for AI Git repos + commit history Traceable evolution history and training data Issues / PRs / Code Review Labeled human decision data Unit tests / CI An automatic grader — AI changes get verified instantly README / API docs / Design Docs Structured context Logs / Traces / Metrics Runtime feedback signals Canary deployment / Rollback A safe trial-and-error mechanism In other words, the software industry spent twenty years turning its production process into a system that is extraordinarily friendly to any external agent — whether that agent is a new hire, an outsourced team, or an AI.
When the coding agent arrived, it didn’t need the software industry to do any extra adapting. It simply plugged into a collaboration infrastructure that had been ready and waiting for it all along.
Knowledge Accumulation Got “Embedded” Into the Production Process This is the single thing the software industry has done that other industries should learn from most.
In many industries, “knowledge management” is something that requires its own project approval, its own budget, its own performance review — someone has to write documentation, maintain the wiki, run training sessions. But because it has no direct link to business output, it’s always the lowest priority, and it never gets done well.
In the software industry, by contrast, knowledge accumulation isn’t an “extra thing” — it’s a byproduct of the production process.
An engineer doesn’t need to make any “extra effort” to do knowledge management. As long as he follows the established practices, knowledge gets automatically captured in a structured form.
Flip the lens to most traditional industries: knowledge lives in people’s heads, in some sheet of some Excel file, in WeChat chat histories. It’s not that knowledge isn’t being produced — it’s that it’s scattered everywhere, exists in all manner of forms, hard to find and hard to read.
The other side of this coin: the reason AI still can’t break into other industries isn’t that the models aren’t strong enough — it’s that those industries haven’t yet prepared themselves to onboard any “external agent” — whether that agent is an AI, or a new employee.
Why Can’t Most Industries Absorb AI? A world without a source of truth.
Dirty, Dirty, Dirty, Dirty Anyone who’s done one or two AI deployment projects in a traditional industry will know these scenes all too well:
Dirty data: numbers don’t match across systems, systems don’t match reality, and nobody knows what a given data field actually means.
Dirty processes: the SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) and manuals are written in meticulous detail, but nothing actually runs the way the flowchart says it does.
Dirty systems: a dozen-plus systems spanning different eras and different vendors — interfaces either don’t exist, or they’re SOAP, or the workflow is “export to Excel and import it again.”
Dirty knowledge: the core know-how lives inside a veteran’s head, in WeChat chat logs, in some unmaintained Excel file nobody owns.
Stack all four kinds of dirty on top of each other, and for AI to actually deliver value becomes nearly impossible.
But the deeper problem isn’t that “things are dirty right now” — it’s that over the past decade-plus, digital transformation didn’t actually solve this. In many places, it made it worse.
Most Turned Into Dashboards Over the past decade, nearly every halfway-respectable industrial enterprise has done “digital transformation,” “Industrial Internet,” “smart factory,” “centralized data platform.” But if you look at what these projects actually delivered, the vast majority produced the same thing: a big screen, a few dashboards, a handful of reports.
The various systems, each abbreviated to an English acronym, all sounded right. The project proposals wrote up smoothly. The acceptance demos all ran fine. But they share one common trait: they don’t change how the enterprise operates — they merely “display” the results of how the enterprise already operates.
And so they all ended up as big-screen dashboards for the boss. Display is not the same as change. It became a kind of digital bureaucracy.
Tools, Processes, Organization Let me try to break down the things in an enterprise that “can be digitized / made intelligent” into three layers.
Tool Layer
What this layer does, fundamentally, is: take things where “people already know what to do” and do them faster, more reliably, cheaper.
The vast majority of “AI projects” and “digital transformation projects” operate at this layer. The reason is simple — they’re easy to get approved, easy to sign off, easy to tell a story about. (I also call this peripheral intelligence.)
Process Layer
Take the links that used to depend on human coordination, meeting-driven decisions, and experience-based judgment, and turn them into system-driven chains with feedback and room for optimization.
The value here is far higher than at the Tool Layer, but it demands data that flows, systems that connect, processes that are explicit, and accountability that is clear. This is where the vast majority of enterprises get stuck — because it requires you to clean up the “four kinds of dirty” first.
Organization Layer
The enterprise itself, as a complex system: how it is described, how it is run, how it is improved.
People can handle local problems by experience, but they struggle to manage a high-dimensional, dynamic, multi-objective, tightly-constrained system in real time. What the third layer aims to do is turn this complex system itself into something that can be modeled, monitored, and tuned.
This is the layer where AI-Native should truly come into play. But it has an awkward characteristic: it’s a hard sell.
If you accept this three-layer framework, one thing becomes clear: what makes AI transformation genuinely hard isn’t the models — it’s the dirty work.
And here’s the problem: nobody wanted to do this dirty work over the past few decades, and it’s hard to imagine anyone wanting to do it in the future. Because it doesn’t win glory, doesn’t earn credit, doesn’t make slides, doesn’t advance careers.
Genuine and Pseudo Tacit Knowledge There’s a widely-circulated saying: “A lot of what we do in this industry is tacit knowledge — AI can’t learn it.”
That’s half right, and half wrong.
Genuine tacit knowledge absolutely exists. But pseudo tacit knowledge is far more common:
Things that could have been written down, but nobody wrote them. Things that could have been structured, but the organization doesn’t reward it. Things that could have been shared, but sharing them means losing personal leverage. A huge amount of what gets called “experience” in vertical industries actually falls into the latter category. Treating pseudo tacit knowledge as genuine tacit knowledge and enshrining it has two results: first, the cost of bringing new people into the field is enormous — the only path is “follow people, watch people, put in the years”; second, AI deployment hits a wall, because what AI feeds on most is accessible, structured, verifiable context.
And the reason the software industry lets newcomers ramp up fast and AI plug in quickly is precisely that it has institutionalized the practice of making explicit what can be made explicit — if you don’t make it explicit, your PR won’t pass, CI won’t run, code review won’t approve. The process forces you to spit your knowledge out.
Behind this are two fundamentally different organizational forms.
The internet and open-source communities are organized around products, systems, collaboration, and reusability — they form a product organization.
Many traditional industries, by contrast, are expert organizations.
Dimension Product Organization Expert Organization Code / work output Code must be readable by others Whoever understands it calls the shots Interfaces / usage Interfaces must be usable by others Whoever has seniority makes the call Role of documentation Documentation must be readable by newcomers Documentation serves audits, not collaboration Problem handling Bugs must be reproducible by others Whoever stepped on the landmine first has authority Module design Modules must be replaceable Whoever controls the equipment, data, and process has the leverage Knowledge flow Knowledge must flow across teams Newcomers learn by following people, not systems Newcomer growth Depends on documentation, processes, systems, and community accumulation Depends on masters, mentors, and experience transmission Source of authority Comes from product quality, system capability, and collaboration efficiency Comes from experience, seniority, resource control, and historical accumulation An expert organization feels “safer” on the surface — because critical capability is locked inside a few people and won’t spiral out of control. But it has one fatal side effect: it is a system closed to outsiders (whether those outsiders are newcomers or agents).
Which leads to an awkward situation: AI can’t get in, because newcomers can’t get in either.
So, Where Is the Bottleneck? Back to the opening question: why can’t most industries absorb AI?
Because over the past few decades, they never built an internal system that is “friendly to outsiders.” This isn’t a problem of “AI can’t get in” — it’s a problem of “no systematic force can get in.” AI has simply shone a fresh light on it.
And the real irony is — the way many industries are investing in AI right now is still piling more “agent”-branded dashboards onto the first layer (the Tool Layer), rather than paying down the debt they’ve accumulated on the second and third layers.
The ROI Trap Most companies, when crunching the numbers on AI, are using an algorithm from a previous era. That algorithm determines that they can see at most a small sliver of AI’s value — and it also dooms their AI transformation to remain stuck at the level of “a cheaper person.”
Three Layers of ROI Here I draw on the framework from The Three Layers of ROI for AI Agents.
Layer 1 ROI: how many people AI replaced, how much time it saved, how much cost it cut.
This is the AI value that the vast majority of companies grasp most easily. Its strength is that it’s intuitive; its weakness is that it has a low ceiling.
Because at its core, it’s about making old processes cheaper and faster — it doesn’t change how the company operates. The role AI plays here is simply that of a “cheaper person.”
Layer 2 ROI: AI enables people to do things they previously couldn’t do, or couldn’t do well.
The value is far higher than Layer 1, but the barrier to entry is also far higher. It requires companies to be willing to spend on “invisible infrastructure.” The vast majority of companies get stuck at this layer. Not because they can’t do the math, but because this investment demands that the CEO have 3-5 years of patience, while most managers operate with a 6-12 month horizon.
Layer 3 ROI: the agent is part of the organization.
Its core question isn’t “what can AI do for me?”, but: what permissions does the agent hold within the organization? At this point, it is no longer just a “tool” — it becomes a new kind of actor in how the organization runs, and the company’s entire way of working must be redesigned around it.
This is what AI-Native really means. Not “we use AI,” but “the organization has redesigned how it works around AI.” The returns are non-linear, compounding, and long-term.
Why Does the Money Always Flow to Layer 1? It’s not that companies can’t see the value of Layer 2 and Layer 3 — it’s that their decision-making mechanisms can only write checks for Layer 1.
This is the most awkward part of AI transformation. The reason Layer 2 and Layer 3 ROI is so high is precisely because they are built on “paying down debt” — cleaning up dirty data, untangling broken processes, capturing scattered knowledge, and connecting isolated systems.
And this “debt” is what every manager over the past several decades racked up in pursuit of their own short-term KPIs.
So in the end, everyone settles on a kind of low-level consensus:
Build an AI system that doesn’t change power structures, doesn’t change business processes, doesn’t challenge existing KPIs — one that can be signed off on, can be demoed, can be written up in reports.
It looks like the company “uses AI,” but nothing about how the company actually operates has changed in any meaningful way.
And so, the vast majority of companies only know how to calculate Layer 1 ROI. They will naturally use AI as a cheaper person. But there’s a fatal problem here — when everyone uses AI as a cheaper person, the market will rapidly erase that value. Advantages from cost reduction are short-term, replicable, and something competitors can catch up to quickly.
The Essence of AI Transformation Isn’t Technical A somewhat uncomfortable conclusion:
The deeper AI transformation goes, the less it becomes a technical problem and the more it becomes a problem of authority, process, and organization.
The Philosophy of X-Ops The essence of turning something into Ops is transforming a complex system into a control system that can receive feedback, self-regulate, and learn.
By this point, the first three sections have laid out the problem with considerable clarity:
The software industry was hit hardest by AI, not because it understands AI best, but because it spent the last two decades building DevOps
Most traditional industries cannot absorb AI, not because AI isn’t powerful enough, but because they have no source of truth
Most enterprises’ AI transformations stall at the first layer, not because the ROI math doesn’t add up, but because their decision-making apparatus can only fund what produces visible ROI
If an industry genuinely wants to absorb AI, what it needs isn’t “AI” — it’s its own flavor of Ops. I call this X-Ops, where X can be any industry that wants to be genuinely transformed by AI.
But X-Ops is not simply “copy DevOps into another industry.” It’s a deeper way of thinking — about how an organization turns itself into a system that can be continuously observed, continuously fed back into, and continuously improved.
A Control-Theoretic Lens: The Organization as a Feedback System The core idea of control theory is remarkably simple: for any system to operate continuously in an uncertain environment and improve itself, it must have a closed loop — sense, decide, act, receive feedback, then return to sensing.
DevOps, in fact, closely resembles a control system.
When you break DevOps apart, it maps almost directly onto a control-theoretic structure:
Control Theory Concept DevOps Counterpart Controlled system Production software systems, infrastructure, user experience Target state SLO (Service Level Objective), business metrics, reliability goals, delivery targets Sensor Logs, metrics, tracing, monitoring, alerting, user feedback Controller CI/CD pipelines, release strategies, platform rules, team decisions Actuator Deployment, rollback, scaling, rate limiting, circuit breaking, feature flags Disturbance Traffic spikes, code defects, dependency failures, user behavior changes Feedback loop Monitor → assess → adjust → observe again Learning mechanism Incident postmortems, continuous improvement, automated test hardening So DevOps is not merely “automated deployment.”
The problem with the traditional development model is that feedback is too slow: requirements go live months later, incidents are only discovered after they happen, and only after ops takes over does development realize the code was never operable.
DevOps fixes this by shortening the feedback chain:
Code commit → automated testing → automated deployment → production observability → user feedback → rapid correction.
This is remarkably similar to negative feedback in control theory: detect the deviation, correct it, and bring the system back within the target range.
At the same time, “stability” in DevOps is not stillness — it is dynamic stability. This point is crucial. Traditional management likes to pursue “no change,” believing that fewer changes bring stability. The DevOps logic is closer to control theory: stability isn’t the absence of change, but remaining controllable amidst continuous change. Or, put another way:
Small-step changes, rapid detection, rapid rollback, rapid recovery, continuous learning.
Now look at the organizational level. The vast majority of companies are not a closed-loop system. They are more like an open-loop pipeline: leadership makes a call → orders are issued → execution happens → … and then nothing.
And AI’s real value is built precisely on top of closed loops. The working style that suits models best is small-step iteration: propose a change, run it, see the results, correct, and go again. If an organization itself lacks a mechanism for “runnable, verifiable, rollback-able,” then AI can’t find its place — it can’t know whether it got things right, and it can’t know where to go next.
So the first meaning of X-Ops is this: transform the organization from an open-loop system into a closed-loop system.
The Three Foundations Control theory provides an abstract lens. To make it concrete, I believe there are three foundations.
The Fact Layer
Does the system have visibility? A source of truth — this doesn’t mean all data must be piped into a data warehouse, nor that all knowledge must become a knowledge graph. But certain things must be established as “the source of fact.” You can describe it in terms of “data lineage” or “ontology,” but the core purpose is the same: solving the problem of AI-ready organizational context. This layer addresses what AI ought to trust.
The Change Record
Does the system have adjustability? AI has no hands. The physical world and the digital world are inherently separated. To prevent the gap between the two from growing ever wider, critical changes must be traceable. This can be loosely compared to Git, but the point isn’t to slap on “Git” — it’s that the act of change itself must be institutionalized. Who changed what? Why? What is the blast radius? This layer addresses whether AI can understand the organization’s evolution.
The Evaluation & Feedback System
Does the system have learnability? For the vast majority of deep learning or AI deployment projects, I’ve consistently insisted on evaluation first: build a test set → define evaluation metrics → design the evaluation methodology → evaluate → optimize → evaluate again. This approach is a universal methodology from data science, originally applied to complex system optimization problems. It generalizes far beyond its original domain.
Building Short Feedback Loops Some will say: “In our industry, safety requirements are too strict, quality demands too high, customer expectations too unforgiving — we can’t iterate fast the way the internet does.”
But the higher-risk the system, the stronger its version management should be, the more rigorous its testing, the more comprehensive its automated verification, the more meticulous its change records. In other words, the harder it is to “iterate fast,” the more an industry needs Ops — not for speed, but for control.
The essence of DevOps has never been “fast.” It’s “still controllable while being fast.” Build your own feedback cadence — it might be monthly, quarterly, annually, but it must be a closed loop.
The Human Use of Human Beings The deepest meaning of X-Ops is redefining the role of human beings within the organization.
In the past, people in organizations primarily played two roles: the executor (the one who does the work) and the decider (the one who calls the shots). The former is responsible for getting things done; the latter is responsible for deciding what to do and how. Before the age of AI, both roles were scarce — execution required skill, judgment required experience.
AI has changed this structure. Execution grows cheaper by the day, and judgment is beginning to be partially taken over by AI.
Looking back at past waves of technological change, technology rarely eliminates human value outright — it more often shifts where value resides. From the Industrial Revolution to AI, the human role has been steadily moving from “directly operating on objects” to “designing, monitoring, and regulating a system that operates on objects.”
At this point, human value hasn’t disappeared — it has moved up a level.
P vs NP offers a metaphor: finding an answer may be extraordinarily hard, but verifying one may be relatively easy. Just as a year ago we were still doing classical programming by hand, today the conversation has shifted to harness engineering — from “I build the thing myself, with my own hands” to “I define the goals, constraints, tests, acceptance criteria, and feedback loops, and let the machine generate results within a controllable range.” The critical position for humans is no longer being the producer of every specific output, but rather the higher-level evaluator, goal-setter, constraint-designer, feedback-loop builder.
Of course, people’s fear of “automation” is more a matter of control and distribution — and in the age of AI, what deepens the fear even further is that it now encroaches on the position of “evaluator” itself. But all of that is a discussion for another day.
The Three Dangers of X-Ops X-Ops sounds like a perfectly correct methodology: make things explicit, make things versioned, build feedback loops, continuously improve. But the more correct something is, the more easily it mutates inside an organization. Especially in large companies and traditional industries, any methodology eventually risks becoming new processes, new systems, new forms — even new bureaucracy.
The first danger is tool worship.
X-Ops is not “buy a set of Ops tools.” If you think bringing in Git and CI/CD means you’ve done X-Ops, you’ve most likely just wrapped a new layer of bureaucratic shell around the same old thing.
The second danger is metric alienation.
The moment you build a measurement system, someone in the organization will start optimizing the metric itself rather than the goal behind it — reward hacking. Goodhart’s Law: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. The goal of X-Ops is to use measurement to drive improvement, not to use measurement to punish people. The moment metrics are used to assign blame, the organization instinctively begins to fabricate data and hide problems, and you’re right back on the well-worn path to digital bureaucracy.
The third danger is the illusion of control.
The most dangerous deviation of all is treating engineering as everything — everything must be measurable, everything must be automatable, everything must be regression-testable. A truly mature X-Ops is the discipline of knowing what can be engineered, and what should not be.
Conclusion Back to the opening question.
Because the question isn’t just whether AI is there — it’s whether the industry itself has become a system that AI can understand, participate in, and optimize.
Otherwise, AI is just a more advanced shell wrapped around the same old way of operating.
Real AI transformation isn’t about putting AI into the enterprise — it’s about turning the enterprise into a system that can run together with AI.
Otherwise, so-called intelligent transformation, in the end, will merely become another form of digital bureaucracy.
summary:
Prologue: From an Old Question to a New One

I remember when I first started out in this industry, I followed a question on Zhihu:

“The internet has been fought to a bloody red ocean, yet smart manufacturing remains a wasteland — why won’t BAT (Baidu, Alibaba, Tencent) step into it?”

Over five years later, this question still has no definitive answer — but the times have changed.

Back then, the subtext was: technology is scarce, industries are waiting to be transformed, and as long as the top-tier players are willing to enter the fray, things will happen. Reality has since falsified that assumption in nearly every way imaginable. And now, LLMs have pushed the “technology is scarce” premise even further. But ask anyone pushing AI adoption in a traditional industry, and odds are they’ll tell you: it’s not that simple.
title: The Hardest Part of AI Transformation Isn't AI — It's the Dirty Work Nobody Wants to Do

Prologue “Have you Harnessed today?”
In 2026, “Harness” is probably the most-used word in the AI agent space — and it comes with enormous misunderstandings. Look back at the past 2–3 years of AI development and you’ll see a mushrooming of agent frameworks, scaffolding, and orchestration tools, most of which sank deep into the mire of technical debt before vanishing without a trace.
In this article, I’m not going to dwell on the grand vision of harness engineering (a term that, in my view, carries the connotation of agents self-iterating and optimizing). Let’s just talk about the harness itself.
This article answers exactly one question: what are the irreplaceable core components of a future-facing, minimalist agent harness?
And, within today’s technical context, how to sidestep the technical debt accumulated over the past two years and choose — or build — the right agent harness.
Build the harness well, and the agent will take care of the rest.
Setting the Record Straight: What Exactly Is a Harness? To understand what a harness really is, we need to do a bit of cyber-archaeology.
In traditional software engineering, a test harness is an automated environment built to execute tests, with a core goal of “de-manualization.”
In the LLM and agent space, harness was initially used more in the context of LLM evaluation. On complex benchmarks like SWE-bench, a bare LLM cannot score highly — it needs an external loop and tools to assist. At this stage, harness was confined to the eval domain.
It wasn’t until applications like Claude Code appeared that agent harness gradually took on a new meaning: the foundation that supports AI work.
The earliest source we can find: on September 23, 2025, developer Vivek Trivedy was the first to systematically articulate “agent harness” in a blog post.
“Agent Harness: External set of functionality to enhance a model’s runtime execution. …
But the concept truly exploded after two articles: Anthropic: Effective harnesses for long-running agents (Nov 26, 2025) and OpenAI: Harness engineering: leveraging Codex in an agent-first world (Feb 11, 2026).
Simply put, in one sentence: a harness is the software shell wrapped around an LLM — it turns a model that can only talk into an agent that can get things done, and even into a long-running agent that operates for hours at a stretch.
Various ecosystems, LangChain included, have been staking claims by inventing a dizzying array of concepts. So what exactly is the difference between agent frameworks, runtimes, scaffolding, and harnesses?
Drawing on LangChain’s article: Agent Frameworks, Runtimes, and Harnesses - oh my!
Runtime: the low-level infrastructure that solves “how an agent runs stably and persistently in production.”
Framework: provides a set of abstractions and programming models to help you “write agent applications more easily.”
Harness: layers default configurations, tools, and workflows on top of a framework, creating a “high-level kit you can grab and run tasks with out of the box.”
(Concepts are all man-made — the thing comes first, the concept follows.)
Design Philosophy: Less is More The lessons distilled from How To Be A World-Class Agentic Engineer and Learning the Bitter Lesson converge on a single idea: Less is More. The various structures and inductive biases humans inject may buy short-term wins, but over the long run they harden into constraints — they should be progressively stripped away as models and compute improve.
Here is the corollary of The Bitter Lesson in the agent space, stated bluntly:
Every line of “smart” control logic you write in your harness is a bet against future model capabilities. Consider:
In 2024 you wrote an elaborate ReAct state machine → In 2025, models natively support multi-turn tool calling, and your state machine becomes dead weight.
In 2025 you wrote a complex planning module → In 2026, models’ reasoning capabilities are strong enough to plan autonomously, and your Planner becomes a ceiling rather than a scaffold.
This is not an argument against having a harness. It is an argument for asking, of every component you put in: “Could the model learn to do this on its own?”
If the answer is “sooner or later,” it is an auxiliary component — write it with a demolition plan from day one. If the answer is “never” (because it touches the physical world outside the LLM), then it qualifies as a core component.
Flipped the other way: minimal, least-necessary components are precisely what make a harness resilient enough to survive the next 3–6 months of model iteration.
Concretely, taking “long-running” as the target, harness components fall into two categories:
Core components: agent loop, context management, atomic core tools, file system, hooks and subagent isolation (or task management)
Auxiliary components: (short-term patches — destined for replacement in the long run)
Core Components Agent Loop Every story about agents begins with the agent loop. A good agent loop is necessarily lean — the LLM drives the decision-making, not a set of pre-scripted paths imposed by humans.
At the lowest level, an LLM is still an autoregressive model that predicts the next token. This fundamental premise holds for the foreseeable future. The Agent Loop does only one thing: execute tools → capture results → append to context → call the LLM again.
def agent_loop(query): messages = [{"role": "user", "content": query}] while True: response = client.messages.create( model=MODEL, system=SYSTEM, messages=messages, tools=TOOLS, max_tokens=8000, ) messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content}) if response.stop_reason != "tool_use": return results = [] for block in response.content: if block.type == "tool_use": output = run_bash(block.input["command"]) results.append({ "type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": block.id, "content": output, }) messages.append({"role": "user", "content": results}) (from learn-claude-code)
This is the core loop of an agent. All the “intelligence” comes from the same place: the LLM’s internal reasoning as it predicts the next token. The harness simply feeds the tool response back in, so that this reasoning process can continue.
Of course, from an engineering and production standpoint, the agent loop is still this while True loop at heart — but it needs a substantial defensive layer to build resilience.
According to unofficial analysis of Claude Code’s source, queryLoop (src/query.ts:241) wraps the core loop with over 1,700 lines of defensive machinery, including:
Streaming handling
Permission checks
Tool format processing and validation
Error recovery
…
Context Management Over the past six-plus months, the term “Context Engineering” has started appearing with increasing frequency. It isn’t about simple prompt tuning — it addresses a more fundamental tension: the contradiction between ever-accumulating messages and the LLM’s finite context window and attention budget. In a long-running scenario, continuously appending messages eventually leads to:
Context overflow: hitting the physical upper bound of the LLM’s context window
Context Rot: staying within the window doesn’t mean the model can process everything effectively — too much irrelevant information can severely degrade the model’s reasoning
How does a well-designed harness handle context?
Context management is not just about splicing and trimming prompts. The central principle is token cost — or more precisely, KV-cache hit rate. In other words: optimize context while preserving cache hits.
1. Microcompact
Cleans up intermediate tool results generated during earlier rounds of tool use. Based on source-code analysis, Claude Code distinguishes two approaches:
Time-based Microcompact: When the user returns after being away for a while, the server-side prompt cache has already expired. There is no cache to protect, so the harness directly constructs new message objects to replace old tool-result content.
Cached Microcompact (Context editing): While the cache is still alive, message content must not be replaced. Instead, the harness uses the Anthropic API’s Context editing mechanism to delete specific tool_result entries at the API level.
2. Summary, Compact, Auto-Compact
When a threshold is triggered — manually or automatically — the harness calls an LLM to summarize and condense the existing context, replacing the previous context with the result. In Claude Code, for example, the summary must include:
// The summary must contain the following 9 sections 1. Primary Request and Intent // The user's main request and intent 2. Key Technical Concepts // Key technical concepts involved 3. Files and Code Sections // Files and code sections touched 4. Errors and fixes // Errors encountered and how they were fixed 5. Problem Solving // Problem-solving steps taken 6. All user messages // All messages from the user 7. Pending Tasks // Tasks still pending 8. Current Work // Work currently in progress 9. Optional Next Step // Suggested next step (optional) In the Anthropic Claude API, the system prompt is independent from Messages, which enables a “full summary” model — the entire message history is replaced with a summary.
By contrast, OpenAI’s Codex uses a layered approach of “initial context + most recent user messages (up to 20k tokens) + summary”:
3. Offload
Unlike compact, which is lossy compression, offload uses the file system to persist context into files for permanent storage, freeing the corresponding context-window space. The offloaded content can be loaded back on demand — this is lossless compression.
4. Isolation
For processes that involve extensive tool calls and exploration, large tasks are decomposed into smaller sub-tasks handled by subagents. This keeps each sub-task’s context independent and isolated, preventing context contamination.
The goal of context management: find the optimal balance among information completeness × KV-cache hit rate × token cost.
Atomic Tools Strictly speaking, what LLMs call tool use isn’t really “using tools.” The model itself is merely outputting tool-call text in a special format; the actual tool execution is carried out by the harness, which parses that text and translates it into real calls against the environment.
But more tools is not better. An excess of tools not only reduces call accuracy but also eats up precious context, causing noticeable Context Rot. For an agent, what really matters isn’t the number of tools — it’s whether the tools possess:
Atomicity
Orthogonality
Completeness
Composability
Put another way, the tool layer should be “primitivized” as much as possible — each tool does exactly one well-defined thing, but these tools can be freely composed to build complex behaviors.
Whether it’s Manus’s three-tier tool design or Anthropic’s proposed tool-search-tool and programmatic tool calling, the core question behind both is the same:
For an agent / LLM, what counts as a “native tool”?
A native tool isn’t a highly specialized interface that engineers wrap for business convenience. It’s a tool form that the model is more familiar with, finds easier to understand, and can invoke more reliably. You could also call them agent-oriented tools.
bash is all you need
Low-level capabilities like Bash, file reads and writes, text editing, and regex processing are the most common operational interfaces in the Linux and software-engineering world. They appear with extremely high frequency in training data, which also makes them far more likely to fall within the training distribution of agentic RL. By contrast, those hand-crafted custom tools — with ambiguous semantics and wildly inconsistent interface styles — often impose a significantly heavier burden on the agent’s understanding and tool-selection process.
So, rather than giving an agent hundreds of highly specialized third-party plugins, it’s better to prioritize a small set of stable, low-level, composable foundational capabilities.
(CRUD for agents?)
File System In an agent system, the file system means far more than just “saving files.”
On one hand, the file system can persist information that would otherwise live only inside the context window — intermediate results, plans, drafts, observation notes, and working memory (i.e., the context offload discussed earlier).
On the other hand, the file system itself is one of the most natural execution environments. Especially in coding-agent scenarios, code, configuration, logs, test output, scripts, and documentation are all organized around files to begin with. Rather than inventing yet another set of memory interfaces or tool abstractions, providing basic operations like read, write, and edit aligns better with how agents actually work — and is closer to real OS-level workflows.
For these reasons, the file system in a harness typically plays at least three critical roles:
Working Memory: stores intermediate state, task plans, incremental conclusions, and temporary artifacts
Execution Environment: hosts code, scripts, configuration, inputs/outputs, and run results
Isolation Boundary / Sandbox: constrains the agent’s operational scope and permissions through controlled directories, virtual file systems, or sandbox environments
The paper Everything is Context: Agentic File System Abstraction for Context Engineering borrows the Unix philosophy of “Everything is a file” and applies it to the agent’s file system.
Subagent The subagent began as a context isolation mechanism. It has since evolved into a fundamental unit of task scheduling and multi-agent coordination.
In many tasks, the main agent’s context is an extremely precious resource. Operations like searching a large codebase, running tests, debugging errors, or performing long-running analysis tend to generate enormous amounts of procedural noise. If all those intermediate steps are stuffed into the main conversation, they not only pollute the primary context but also degrade the quality of subsequent reasoning and decision-making.
The natural solution, then, is: spawn a subagent and let it handle the “dirty work” in an isolated context.
The main conversation doesn’t need to consume the full process — it only needs to receive a summarized result. From the main agent’s perspective, it simply called a higher-order tool — an AgentTool — and then waits for (or later receives) a block of summary text, which it writes back into its own context as a tool_result. In other words, introducing subagents does not alter the structure of the core queryLoop.
Zooming out further, the subagent is also a task-scheduling mechanism.
This scheduling generally takes two forms:
Synchronous subagent: called like a regular tool, blocking until the result arrives
Asynchronous subagent: runs as a background task; the main conversation continues forward, and the result is collected later
Once you enter asynchronous mode, the system must maintain a global task registry (e.g., AppState.tasks in Claude Code), running in the background, and inject completed results back into the main agent’s agent loop as a message.
When multiple background subagents run concurrently, the subagent’s value is no longer just context isolation — it also becomes the starting point for multi-agent orchestration.
Hooks To keep the agent loop lean, new capabilities should not keep piling into the loop itself.
Otherwise, a cycle that was originally just four steps — “execute tools → capture results → append to context → call the LLM again” — quickly gets dragged into a giant if-else state machine by all manner of business logic.
The point of hooks is to move that complexity outside the loop.
Through events like PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PreCompact, SubagentStop, and others, the harness can capture state at key inflection points, inject context, intercept risks, and validate results — without requiring the loop itself to understand every rule.
So the essence of hooks isn’t “a few more extension points.” It’s separating mechanism from policy:
The loop is responsible for advancing execution
Hooks are responsible for exerting control
On the surface, hooks just give you a list of event names. But more importantly, these events define the critical boundaries within an agent system.
Once the boundaries are clear, many capabilities no longer require direct modification of the loop itself — they can be injected as external policies:
Security policies can hook around tool calls
Memory governance can hook around compact operations
Task orchestration can hook onto the subagent lifecycle
…
Boundary Components Beyond the six core components discussed above, there are a handful of concepts that come up often in conversation. Not every one of them is essential, but understanding where they sit helps you avoid over-engineering.
Checkpointing / Recovery
For a long-running agent, error recovery and state checkpointing genuinely matter. But from the harness’s perspective, checkpointing is more of a cross-cutting capability that spans multiple components: the file system handles persistence, hooks trigger saves at key milestones, and the agent loop resumes from the breakpoint. It’s hard to isolate checkpointing as a standalone “component” — it’s really the emergent result of several core components working together.
Planner / Todo-list
Remember in early 2026, Claude Code dropped its built-in todo-list tool. The reason was straightforward: the reasoning capability of the new model generation had become strong enough that an explicit, manually maintained todo list was no longer necessary. This is a perfect echo of The Bitter Lesson — the planning module you write today is quite likely the first auxiliary component to be ripped out tomorrow. If you absolutely must build one, make sure it can be cleanly detached rather than deeply coupled to the loop.
Skills
Skills are essentially a composite built from core components: a command registry, on-demand message injection, and a pile of markdown files. They don’t belong to the harness’s lower layer — they sit in the application layer above it.
MCP / Plugins
MCP is a protocol layer, not part of the harness itself. Supporting MCP is certainly a plus, but what it solves is “how to expose external resources,” not “what core capabilities a harness should have.”
Memory
Memory is undeniably important, but as of today the industry hasn’t converged on a canonical form. Whether you look at OpenClaw or Claude Code, the prevailing approach is a lightweight memory scheme based on markdown files. This “files-as-memory” approach may seem crude, but it aligns perfectly with the harness’s minimalist philosophy — instead of building an elaborate memory database, just let the agent manage its own long-term state through the file interface it already knows best.
Agent Team and Multi-agent
When you scale out to multiple agents, the harness components inside each agent stay exactly the same — the same agent loop, the same context management, the same atomic tools. The only new addition is the connection mechanism between agents.
If your harness is well-designed for a single agent, 90% of the infrastructure can be reused directly when you extend to a team. The difference between a subagent and an agent team member can be summarized in the table below:
Dimension Subagent Agent Team Member Lifecycle Task complete → destroyed Survives across tasks, may run for hours Identity Anonymous (only a task description) Has a role identity (“reviewer”, “coder”, “tester”) State Stateless, vanishes after returning results Has persistent state (its own context history, memory files) Spawn method Spawned by the main agent Pre-defined or dynamically spawned From the harness’s point of view, a multi-agent system requires a few additional things:
Agent registry: who is running, what is their role, current status
Lifecycle hooks: on_spawn, on_idle, on_terminate
Graceful shutdown and resource cleanup: this is the most overlooked — and most critical — piece
A subagent is unidirectional (main agent dispatches a task → subagent returns a result), whereas an agent team needs bidirectional or even multi-directional communication. But the design of that communication should still follow the principle of “fit the LLM’s native habits”: a shared file system is, itself, the most natural communication channel.
Harness Engineering = Bigger Loop Due to space constraints, I won’t go deep into harness engineering here. Borrowing the perspective from What Harness Engineering Discusses: A Unified Framework of Three Scaling Dimensions, the core keyword of harness engineering is scalability — extending across time, expanding across space, and evolving interaction paradigms.
From my perspective, I personally consider harness engineering and compound engineering to be synonyms. The real value lies in: how do you make an agent run better and better over time?
The prerequisite for all of this is a usable harness plus a bigger loop.
A complete agent system has two loops:
Inner loop (agent loop): the “tool call → result capture → context append → next decision” cycle within a single task. This is the primary focus of this article.
Outer loop (evolution loop): the “execute → evaluate → feedback → improve” cycle across tasks. This is the primary focus of harness engineering.
You can see these ideas at play in Andrej Karpathy’s autoresearch and in Ralph Loop.
The outer loop doesn’t need a separate architecture — it’s the inner loop’s core components, reused on a larger time scale. Hooks provide observation points, the file system provides persistent memory, subagents provide parallel experimentation capability, and context management ensures runs don’t spiral out of control.
Design Principles for a Good Harness 1. Minimality
If a component isn’t strictly necessary, don’t add it. For every component, ask yourself:
“If I remove this, does the agent stop working entirely — or does it just run a little less well?”
The former is core. The latter is auxiliary.
2. Removability
Auxiliary components must be cleanly removable, not entangled with core components.
This is The Bitter Lesson applied to engineering: whatever you write today,
must be easy for your future self, six months later, to delete cleanly.
3. Model Agnosticism
The harness should not bake in special-case adaptations for any particular model’s quirks.
A good harness: swap the model, change the API call, and it runs.
(Prompt optimization for a specific model is a different matter, of course.)
4. Evolution-Ready
The ultimate goal of a harness is not “run a task,”
but “provide an environment where the agent gets better over time.”
Every component’s design should answer:
How does it contribute data, feedback, or tunable parameters to the outer loop?
Conclusion An agent harness does not get better as it gets more complex. Quite the opposite — its value lies in restraint.
The history of agents from 2023 to 2026 is, at its core, a history of human-designed control logic being continuously outcompeted by model capabilities. The real mission of a harness is to provide the model with a stable, safe, evolvable runtime environment — and then get out of the way, handing the decisions back to the model itself.

summary:
Prologue

“Have you Harnessed today?”

In 2026, “Harness” is probably the most-used word in the AI agent space — and it comes with enormous misunderstandings. Look back at the past 2–3 years of AI development and you’ll see a mushrooming of agent frameworks, scaffolding, and orchestration tools, most of which sank deep into the mire of technical debt before vanishing without a trace.

In this article, I’m not going to dwell on the grand vision of harness engineering (a term that, in my view, carries the connotation of agents self-iterating and optimizing). Let’s just talk about the harness itself.
title: On Agent Harness: A Minimalist Design Guide for AI Agent Harnesses in 2026

content:
克里斯坦森在《创新者的窘境》中提出了一个反直觉的洞见：良好的管理本身可能就是导致失败的原因。
这个看似悖论的判断，在我亲历的 AI 转型浪潮中得到了残酷的验证。当一个传统 IT 组织试图拥抱 AI 时，它往往不是在做真正的创新，而是在用最先进的技术维护最陈旧的官僚体系。
这不是技术问题，这是组织结构与价值体系的深层冲突。
一、延续性创新与颠覆性创新 克里斯坦森将创新分为截然不同的两类：
**延续性创新 (Sustaining Innovation)：**大公司最擅长的。它的目的是把现有产品做得更好，满足现有的高端客户——因为这些客户愿意支付更高的溢价，能带来最高的利润率。 **颠覆性创新 (Disruptive Innovation)：**刚出现时通常被认为是低端或劣质的。它的性能往往不如主流产品，利润率极低，市场也很小；但在更便宜、更简单、更小巧、更方便等方面具有优势。 窘境在于：管理者为了对股东负责、为了追求利润最大化，理智地决定不投资颠覆性技术，继续专注于延续性创新。然而，正是这个正确的决定，导致了公司错失未来，最终走向衰亡。
这不是错误，而是理性的诅咒。
AI 对于传统 IT 来说，正是这样一种颠覆性创新。
二、价值观的冲突 传统 IT 和 AI 研发遵循的是两套完全不同的价值体系，这种冲突在克里斯坦森看来并非偶然，而是根植于价值网络的内在逻辑。
价值网络决定了企业的成本结构、所需规模以及必须实现的基本增长率。当企业在某个特定的网络内逐渐积累经验，它会形成符合该网络独特要求的能力、组织结构和文化。IT 部门诞生于一个追求稳定、可预测的价值网络，而 AI 则需要完全不同的生存土壤。
这种冲突首先体现在资源分配逻辑上。克里斯坦森指出，在经营状况良好的企业中，“客户有效地控制了资源分配模式”。良好的资源分配流程的设计初衷就是淘汰得不到客户认可的提案——这正是大企业的经营之道。但问题是，破坏性技术的最终用户或应用领域是无法预知的，“你无法对尚不存在的市场进行分析”。当一个 AI 项目的 ROI 无法被现有客户价值网络所验证时，它就会在资源分配中被系统性淘汰。
更深层的冲突在于组织能力本身。“一个机构的能力独立于机构内部工作人员的能力而存在，“克里斯坦森写道，“一个机构的能力体现在其流程和价值观中，而且正是构成当前业务模式核心能力的流程和价值观，决定了它们无力应对市场的破坏性变化。“IT 部门引以为傲的零事故流程、层层审批机制、供应商管理制度——这些构成其核心能力的要素，恰恰成为拥抱 AI 创新的枷锁。
当 IT 部门被赋予 AI 转型的使命时，它本能地会用自己熟悉的方式来管理：制定标准、建立流程、控制风险。但这恰恰是扼杀 AI 创新的方式。
IT 追求稳定，AI 需要混乱；IT 害怕失败，AI 拥抱试错；IT 看重合规，AI 追求突破。
三、用 20 世纪的规则管理 21 世纪的技术 传统 IT 部门面对 AI 时，会本能地套用既有的管理流程。但这些流程往往成为创新的绊脚石。
一个真实的场景：
DeepSeek 刚发布 R1 时，试图导入 671B 模型权重进行内部测试。IT 部门的回复是：这是"新型业务需求”，需要从业务和安全角度进行全方位评估。等流程走完——风险评估、合规审查、供应商准入、数据出境审查——这个模型可能已经更新了两个版本。
这不是某个人的错。在他们的价值体系里，这是负责任的体现。但 AI 的价值体系完全不同。
正如 Steam, Steel, and Infinite Minds 文章里提及的1865 年英国红旗法案： 那时汽车刚刚出现，法律规定每辆汽车前面必须有人举着红旗步行开道， 结果汽车速度被限制得比马车还慢。用旧制度管理新技术，最后把新技术管成了旧技术的附庸。
克里斯坦森在书中写过一个令人警醒的结论：“在单纯追求利润和增长率的过程中，一些优秀企业的优秀管理者因为使用了最佳管理技巧而导致了企业的失败。”
这不是讽刺，而是结构性困境。当企业的价值网络（利润结构、客户期望、渠道利益、成本模型）完全固化后，即使管理者做出当时情境下最理性的决策，也会把组织锁死在延续性创新的轨道上。
技术迭代的速度已经超出了传统管理框架能够消化的范围。这不是局部摩擦，而是系统性的不可调和。
四、破局 克里斯坦森在书中给出的解决方案是：独立。
既然大公司的流程和价值观注定会排斥颠覆性创新，那么颠覆性创新必须在组织之外独立孵化。
对于传统企业的 AI 转型，这意味着：
不要试图在现有的 IT 架构内孵化 AI 团队。 必须给予 AI 团队独立的预算、独立的决策权、独立的价值观。 允许他们满足于小市场、低利润、高风险——这在传统 IT 体系内是无法生存的。 更深层的启示是：外部市场的压力会逼迫你进化，而内部保护伞只会让你退化。
如果一个企业真的想做好 AI，可能需要接受一个反直觉的事实：
与其把 AI 团队放在 IT 部门里慢慢窒息，不如让他们独立出去，哪怕这意味着失去控制。
结语 《创新者的窘境》写于互联网早期，但它对 AI 时代的预言几乎是精准的。
传统 IT 做不好 AI，不是因为技术不够先进，而是因为它的管理太成功了——它用最高效的方式维护着最不适合创新的体系。
这不是 IT 部门的失败，而是组织结构对颠覆性创新的必然排斥。理解这一点，或许能帮助更多企业在 AI 浪潮中少走弯路。
毕竟，理性的诅咒，只有当你意识到它存在时，才有可能打破它。
